coredata.dc:title: Is precarbonization necessary for effective laser graphitization?
coredata.dc:creator: Yadav Kumar, Manish
Borenstein, Arie
coredata.dc:description: Laser-graphitization offers a new approach to obtaining high-quality 3D graphene films simply and efficiently. Over the years this method was developed to include different carbonaceous precursors and irradiation parameters. Precarbonization is a step in the synthesis where the precursors are annealed at high temperatures under an inert atmosphere to increase the carbon content in the samples prior to the laser reaction. Some studies report this step while others do not, thus its necessity and beneficially have to be evaluated. In this study, we compare the laser products of as-prepared and precarbonized carbon nanodots (CNDs). The study investigates the samples from the initial synthesis step of microwave-assisted formation of CNDs, through separation of the CNDs by particle size, the precarbonization step conducted at different temperatures, to the final 3D graphene films products. By analyzing the chemical structure and graphitization level of the intermediates, we could realize the chemical characteristics responsible for successful laser-graphitization. We have found that precarbonization indeed leads to improved graphitic products, only to a certain extent. The best graphitization level is achieved when the CNDs were precarbonized to 300 °C obtaining ID/IG ratio = 0.44 as compared to 1.08 when as-prepared CNDs are used. On the other hand, when annealed to 400 °C the laser produces more defected graphene films (ID/IG ratio = 0.83). Moreover, both the surface chemistry and the core structure of the CNDs affect the final results. A careful examination of the surface chemistry reveals the coverage of oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups required for the optimal thermochemical reaction.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the Israeli Ministry of Energy for supporting this research via grant no. 219-11-133 .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis processes of the different samples compared in this study. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

CNDs analysis. a. particles size obtained by DLS. b. FTIR analysis. c. UVvis spectra of CND; inset: light and UV photos of CNDs. d. XRD pattern of typical CNDs. e. Deconvoluted C1s peaks from XPS spectra of CND-S and CND-P. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Precarbonization. a. UV–vis adsorption of annealed and as prepared CND-P. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.) b. TGA analysis of CND-P annealed at different temperatures. c. Raman spectra of annealed and as prepared CND-P. d. high-resolution C1s XPS peak of annealed and as prepared CND-P. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Laser-reduced rCND. a. Raman spectra of rCND-S with different annealing temperatures. b. Raman spectra of rCND-P with different annealing temperatures. c. Size comparison of CND-P0 and its laser product rCND-P300. D. Typical 3D graphitic matrix of rCND-P300 as seen in SEM images. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Is precarbonization necessary for effective laser graphitization?

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Sciences, Ariel University, Ariel, Israel
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Sciences, Ariel University, Ariel, Israel

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Laser-graphitization offers a new approach to obtaining high-quality 3D graphene films simply and efficiently. Over the years this method was developed to include different carbonaceous precursors and irradiation parameters. Precarbonization is a step in the synthesis where the precursors are annealed at high temperatures under an inert atmosphere to increase the carbon content in the samples prior to the laser reaction. Some studies report this step while others do not, thus its necessity and beneficially have to be evaluated. In this study, we compare the laser products of as-prepared and precarbonized carbon nanodots (CNDs). The study investigates the samples from the initial synthesis step of microwave-assisted formation of CNDs, through separation of the CNDs by particle size, the precarbonization step conducted at different temperatures, to the final 3D graphene films products. By analyzing the chemical structure and graphitization level of the intermediates, we could realize the chemical characteristics responsible for successful laser-graphitization. We have found that precarbonization indeed leads to improved graphitic products, only to a certain extent. The best graphitization level is achieved when the CNDs were precarbonized to 300 °C obtaining ID/IG ratio = 0.44 as compared to 1.08 when as-prepared CNDs are used. On the other hand, when annealed to 400 °C the laser produces more defected graphene films (ID/IG ratio = 0.83). Moreover, both the surface chemistry and the core structure of the CNDs affect the final results. A careful examination of the surface chemistry reveals the coverage of oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups required for the optimal thermochemical reaction.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanomaterials have attracted increasing attention in the last decades due to their unique and tunable physical and chemical properties. Nanocarbons serve a wide range of applications, including energy systems, catalysis, sensors, separation, and purification, and much more [1]. Graphene is probably the most famous carbon allotrope, exhibiting extraordinary conductivity, mechanical strength, surface area, and unique chemical and optical properties [2–6]. However, the complex and expensive fabrication of graphene has been a continuous obstacle, preventing its full employment in commercial-scale applications [7]. Recently, laser-processing of carbonaceous precursors has emerged as a simple, scalable, and cost-effective method to achieve highly graphitized 3D films [8–12]. Beyond the traditional graphene oxide (GO) and polyimides (PI), new carbonaceous materials are being investigated as precursors for laser-assisted fabrication. One such nanocarbon materials are carbon nanodots (CNDs), first proved to form 3D graphene films by laser in 2017 by Strauss et al. [13–15] Carbon nanodots are spherical graphitic particles with a mean size of less than 10 nm. The structure of CNDs varies with the number of layers, graphene fragments, hetero-atoms, and surface chemistry [16,17]. Carbon nanodots cores mainly consist of sp2/sp3domains in various crystallinity. Various functional groups on the surface are attached to carbon nanodots, such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxylic groups [18,19]. These functional groups on the surface of CNDs may be rich in oxygen-containing groups and are favorable for water solubility and further functionalization for various applications. The unique size and structure of CNDs make them interesting candidate for laser graphitization agents. Various synthesis methods are employed to prepare the carbon precursor used in this study, CNDs. These methods include hydrothermal treatments, ultrasonic reactions, polymerization, of different organic precursors [20–22] as well as arc discharge, laser ablation, electrochemical etching, and chemical oxidation [23–25]. We choose microwave-assisted synthesis of citric acid and urea as a standard method for carbon nanodots.

Several recent publications reported an intermediate step prior to laser irradiation, sometimes called precarbonization [26–29]. Yet, other publications do not include this step as necessary for successful laser-conversion of the carbon precursors into graphene [30–33]. Additional synthesis step, which involves heating under an inert atmosphere for several hours, has remarkable consequences on the process's complexity and cost. Recent publications evaluated the effect of precarbonization on other carbonbased materials [34,35]. This study systematically investigates the effect of the precarbonization step on the final product of the laser-reduced graphitic films. X-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), dynamic light scattering (DLS) experiments were performed to compare and characterize the samples. With adequate chemical analysis we hope to be able to characterize the optimal chemical profile of the precursors that leads to the desired results.

CND synthesis Urea and citric acid were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and used for carbon nanodot (CND) synthesis without any further purification. CND was synthesized by mixing citric acid (2 gm) and urea (2 gm), dissolved in deionized water (3 ml). The reaction mixture in a beaker is heated in a microwave (700 W) at a controlled temperature and pressure until the complete evaporation of the solvent. Subsequently, DI water was added to the product to separate the particle of carbon nanodot by mass in centrifugation (5000 rpm for 20 min). This washing process was repeatedly done 4–5 times until a clear solution was achieved. During the centrifugation process, the precipitate part containing the bigger particles (CND-P) and the supernatant part containing the smaller particles (CND-S) were separated and dried overnight at 120 °C. After drying both samples were grinded into a fine powder form with the help of a mortar and pestle for further experimental study.

Annealing Carbon nanodots were thermalized at different temperatures 300 °C and 400 °C in a tube furnace by keeping the synthesized CND inside a Quartz vessel under N2 gas flow providing an inert atmosphere. In case of annealing at 300 °C furnace temperature was raised from room temperature to 250 °C in 60 min after that furnace was heated up to the final temperature 300 °C very slowly with a heating rate of 2 °C min−1 and kept at 300 °C for 120 min. In Case of annealing at 400 °C to reach final temperature, the furnace was heated in two steps, at first, we increased the temperature to 350 °C in 90 min after that again very slowly at the heating rate 2 °C min−1 raised to a final temperature of 400 °C and kept for 120 min.

Laser graphitization all samples were converted into graphitic films with the help of laser irradiation. First the dispersions were sonicated to achieve homogeneous mixtures. Doctor blading method was used to apply slurries on glass substrate followed by drying on hotplate at 70 °C overnight. This homogeneous film was irradiated with laser and brownish color CND converted into graphitic film.

Instrumentation Synthesized carbon nanodots were annealed by furnace KSL-1100X equipped with Vacuubrand 1C to achieve underpressure inside the tube furnace. RADWAG Wagl Elektroniczne instrument was used to measure the weight of all the samples Elemental analysis was done by a thermo-scientific BARGAL analytical instrument. UV–vis spectra were measured by JASCO V-750 Spectrophotometer Instrument. Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Varian Cary Eclipse Fluorescence spectrophotometer. To analyze the mass reduction with temperature thermogravimetric analysis was performed on METTLER TOLEDO TGA/DSC 1 equipped with Gas Controller GC 100 STARe System open to air. To analyze powder X-ray diffraction pattern of carbon nanodot X'Pert Pro X-ray powder diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.154 nm) were used. SEM images were taken by Scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, Tescan MAIA3). Fourier Transform Infrared spectra were measured by using JASCO FT/IR-4700 spectrometer, carbon nanodots size obtained by particle analyser instrument Litesizer500, Anton Paar. Raman spectra were taken with an X PLORA ONETM micro Raman system (Horiba Scientific, Japan) using a Kratos AXIS-HA spectrometer with 532 nm laser power, using a monochromatized Al Kα source. XPS spectra were recorded using a Kratos AXIS Ultra DLD spectrometer equipped with monochromator Al Kα X-ray source (hn 1/4 1486.6 eV).

The electrical conductivity measurements were performed before and after laser treatment, on three different electrodes for each sample. Each electrodes were tested at three points for statistical accuracy. The sample ink was loaded on glass with four contact points, made by silver paste, 1 cm apart from each other. After drying (and lasering, in the case of rCND samples) we measured the electrical conductivity using simple ohmmeter with direct contact on the silver contacts points. Calculations conducted according to:
Conductivity = 1/ ρ(resistivity)
Where Resistance(R) = (ρ* Length)/Cross section area. Using these parameters: Length 1 cm = 0.01 m; Cross-section area = 10-9 m2; Thickness of rCND film 1 μm = (1 × 10-6 m) Width of silver paint 1 mm = 10-3 m).

To estimate the necessity of precarbonization, 3D graphitic films were prepared from CNDs. Microwave-synthesized CNDs were separated into two groups: (I) small particles that remained in the supernatant after centrifugation (CND-S) and (II) large particles that precipitated in centrifugation (CND-P). The effect of annealing was investigated on three different synthetic routes of CNDs (a) without annealing (CND-0), (b) annealed at 300 (CND-300), and (c) at 400 (CND-400). After laser treatments, the final products (rCND) were compared to evaluate their chemical composition, structure, and graphitization level. Fig. 1 schematically illustrates the synthesis processes of the different samples evaluated in this study.

The CNDs were prepared in a domestic microwave. The produced CND dispersions contain a mixture of different types of carbon dots, as proved in previous publications [31]. Carbon nanodots can be separated into two main groups by mass. Subsequent centrifugations are applied until the solutions remain colorless, ensuring complete removal of the suspended particles. Out of 4 g of organic precursors (urea and citric acid, 1:1, w/w) we obtained approximately 650 mg of a precipitant product (Mass yields for each step along the synthesis process are presented in Table S1). After centrifugation, the smaller carbon dots (CND-S, mean 2.2 nm), remain suspended in the colloidal solution while larger particles precipitate (CND-P, mean diameter 332 nm, Fig. 2 a). Both samples contain carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and hydrogen, with similar chemical compositions. Elemental analysis measurements reveal that carbon is the main component (43.95–41.87%) with similar nitrogen and oxygen contents (Table S2). Nevertheless, the surface chemistry of the two CND products is different. CND-S contain more oxygen and nitrogen functional groups as detected by FTIR (Fig. 2b, see detailed analysis in supporting information). The different surface chemistry affects their optical properties as well. UV–vis spectra of CND-s reveal two absorption peaks at 340 and 405 nm related to n-star transition of CO bond of citrazinic acid, and to pyridine ring NHCO, respectively as presence in HTTP molecules [36]. This photoabsorption induces fluorescence peak at 500 nm (Fig. S1). The absorption of CND-P is much lower as a result of a smaller concentration of functional groups. The peak at 405 nm (and the related fluorescence) is even weaker than that of 340 nm due to the lack of nitrous groups on its surface. The difference in fluorescence properties is clearly seen under UV-light. Naturally, the functional groups enable CND-S higher solubility in aqueous solutions (Fig. 2c, inset). Comparing the crystalline structure of the two products by XRD reveals much higher graphitic crystallinity in CND-P (Fig. 2d). The peak at 2θ = 26⁰ attributed to (002) plane of graphene is much sharper than in CND-S, denoting that larger particles are more graphitic. The broad peak for CND-S indicates a more amorphous structure.

The difference in the surface analysis is detected in XPS as well. While the nitrogen and oxygen percentages in the bulk are similar, as measured in elemental analysis, the composition of the surface differs. CND-P contain more carbon on its surface than.

CND-S (75% and 66%, respectively, Fig. S2. See full spectra in Fig. S3). This result proves the higher concentration of functional groups on the surface of CND-S as concluded by the optical properties of the particles. Moreover, deconvolution of the C1s peaks of the two particles confirms the different structures (Fig. 2e). The C1s peak of CND-P consists of more sp2 carbon than that of CND-S (35% and 29%, respectively, Fig. S2). Overall, XPS data prove the two variations between the smaller and larger CND particles: First, CND-S have more heteroatoms, functional groups, on its surface. Second, the CND-P crystalline structure is more graphitic.

Concluding the chemical analysis of the first synthesis step, we can deduce that the microwave-synthesized CNDs can be divided into two main products according to their mass. They differ from each other by their surface chemistry and their graphitic structure. While CND-S have more functional groups on their surface, the CND-P are consisted of larger graphitic crystallites.

Previously, CNDs were reported to be suitable precursors for laser-graphitization, resulting in a unique 3D graphitic macro-structure. Different publications pointed out that a thermal treatment, also called precarbonization, is essential for successful laser conversion of the carbon precursors into graphene. In this study, we want to assess the effect of precarbonization as an intermediate step in the final laser products. To evaluate the impact of precarbonization, as-prepared CNDs are compared with CNDs that were annealed at 300 °C and 400 °C under inert atmosphere, labeled CND-P0, CND-P300, and CND-P400. The CND samples were weighed before and after annealing. The mean mass loss caused by annealing at 300 °C is 9.5% (±1.47%), while at 400 °C 19.57% of the sample mass is reduced (±2.03%, see Table S3, Yields of CND-S annealed at different temperatures are presented in Table S4). The mass loss is in accordance with the heteroatoms elimination. Elemental analysis results are summarized in Table S2. Upon thermal treatment, the carbon content increases on the expense of nitrogen and oxygen elimination. These elemental changes occur both on CND-S and CND-P. For example, carbon percentage before annealing in CND-P0 is 43.95% and increases to 54.94% in CND-P400. A dramatic change is recorded for oxygen contents, decreasing from 35.64% to 30.21% in CND-P300 and further to 23.73% in CND-P400.

The elimination of heteroatoms changes the surface of the carbon particles as well, leading to lower light absorption and fluorescence (Fig. 3 a and Fig. S4). Importantly, the absorption and fluorescence of CNDs annealed at 400 °C are even lower than those annealed at 300 °C. Accordingly, the solubility of CND-P400 is lower than CND-P300. In fact, CND-P400 are not dissolved in water even after thorough ultrasonication. Both samples are still soluble in NMP or DMSO. The hydrophobicity developed after annealing should be attributed to the elimination of hydrophilic functional groups. The change in chemical composition is obtained in thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) as well. The general thermo-stability pattern of all CND-P samples is similar (Fig. 3b and Table S5). However, CND-P300 show higher stability than unannealed CND-P0. CND-P400 are the most thermally stable samples. The thermal stability should be attributed to fewer functional groups on the surface that tend to decompose at lower temperatures than the core particle. Meanwhile, the optical behavior of CND-S samples follows the same trend, with lowering of absorption intensities upon annealing at higher temperatures (Fig. S5). FTIR spectra of CND-S annealed at different temperatures show decrease in nitrogen-containing functional groups, reconfirming the effect of the thermal treatment (Fig. S6). We should mention that carbonization, which means increasing carbon content, is the desirable consequence of the thermal treatment and aims to assist the final synthesis step the laser graphitization.

Another important property of the CND, as a precursor for laser-graphitization, is its graphitization level. The prime tool to evaluate the graphitization of carbon materials is Raman spectroscopy. While as-prepared CNDs are fluorescent and cannot be adequately measured by Raman (see CND-S0 in Fig. S7), due to their intense fluorescence, annealed CNDs fluorescence decreases, allowing informative Raman measurements. The Raman spectra of CND-P0, CND-P300 and CND-P400 is presented in Fig. 3c. The two major peaks, D-band correspond to defects in the graphitic lattice, and G-band, attributed to graphitic sp2 carbon are seen in the Raman spectra. Although the peaks in all samples are broad, indicating only partial graphitization, the peaks in the annealed CNDs are sharper. The graphitic level is typically evaluated by ID/IG ratio. The ID/IG ratios clearly show an increasing graphitization of the larger particles, CND-P. However, no significant effect of annealing on the graphitic level is observed. All three samples, namely CND-P0, CND-P300 and CND-P400 show similar ID/IG.

This is further confirmed by XPS spectroscopy, indicating a decrease of oxygen containing groups on the surface, where oxygen decreases from 17.07% to 6.06% after annealing (Atomic percentage. See Table S6. Full spectra in Fig. S8). Importantly, and perhaps surprisingly, the nitrogen percentage does not decrease but increases, like carbon on the expense of oxygen. This may indicate that the nitrogen atoms are present inside the core structure of the N-doped CND, which is less affected by heating than oxygen-containing functional groups. These results are reconfirmed by elemental analysis (Table S2). XPS can reveal the graphitization level of the carbon on the surface. Deconvoluted C1s peaks, presented in Fig. 3d, indicate that the carbon atoms in the CNDs are less oxidized in CND-P300 as compared to CND-P400 and CND-P0 (68.2%, 62.4%,57.5%, percentage of C–C out of all carbon bonds, respectively).

Summarizing the precarbonization characterization, we can conclude that the thermal treatment on CNDs has no considerable effect on the graphitic structure of both the small and large particles. On the other hand, the surface chemistry of the particles is dramatically affected. The concentration of oxygen and nitrogen is reduced while functional groups are eliminated by annealing.

The final synthesis step is laser irradiation, on which the carbonaceous precursors are converted into graphitic matrix. In this study, we evaluate the effect of precarbonization on the properties of the laser reduction products, rCND. In order to evaluate the precarbonization on the laser-processing final products, pristine and annealed CNDs (with and without annealing treatment) were incorporated in slurries (see experimental section). The slurries were applied on a glass substrate and dried overnight. The dry CND films were then subjected to laser irradiation, as described in Fig. 1. The thermal reaction induced by the laser is seen in bare eyes, as the color of the film changes from brown to black, typical to graphene. The laser initiates a rapid and exothermic thermal reaction, seen as a bright red flash the mass loss during the energetic laser reaction is summarized in Table S7. The mass loss by laser irradiation of the annealed samples is lower than that of the unannealed samples, from 41.55% of CND-P0 down to 39.31 and 32.44% for CND-P300 and CND-P400, respectively. This should be attributed to the elimination of heteroatoms remain in higher quantities in the CND-P0 samples.

The graphitization of the laser-reduced films is analyzed by Raman spectroscopy. Here we noticed a significant difference between the rCND-S and rCND-P products. The laser does not convert the smaller particles, rCND-S, into graphitic film (Fig. 4 a). In this case, annealing does not affect the process and rCND-S300 and rCND-S400 do not show clear D- or G-bands. Moreover, ID/IG ratio is not improved in the annealed particles and is similar for all rCND-S. Contrarily, Raman spectra of rCND-P show high graphitization (Fig. 4b and Table S8). It is very indicative to examine the difference between the annealed and as-prepared particles. Comparing Raman spectra of rCND-P0 and rCND-300 reveals that the precarbonization step enhanced the graphitization process occurred by the laser. Importantly, the ID/IG is improved from 1.08 for rCND-P0 to 0.44 for rCND-P300, which is the best results monitored in this study. This result is the end goal of this research, proving the advantageous precarbonization step. Its consequences and full meaning will be discussed in the Discussion section.

The effect of the laser irradiation can be clearly seen in XPS analysis (Fig. S9). Carbon content on the surface increases in all samples, for example from 72.16 to 80.23 for rCND-P300 (see also intensities in Fig. S9 top). In addition, the carbon is becoming more graphitic and less oxidized, in agreement with the lowering of oxygen and nitrogen of approximately 60% (see intensities in Fig. S9 bottom). High resolution of the N 1s peak reveals the structure surrounding the nitrogen atoms before and after laser treatment. . All N 1s peaks resemble a dissent gaussian shape around binding energy of pyridinic nitrogen (∼400) and experience an average upshifting of.
0.499 eV towards more graphitic nitrogen. This phenomenon is not observed in rCND-P0, indicating inferior graphitization, with accordance to the observation in Raman spectroscopy.

Further characterization techniques are employed to complete the identification of the successful production of graphitic matrix. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of graphitic film shows more crystalline nature (2Ɵ = 26) after laser treatment(See Fig. S10 bottem). To verify this electrical conductivity measurements were performed before and after laser treatment to investigate the effect of precarbonization on three different electrodes for each sample (See Table S9, Fig. S11). We observed higher value of resistance in case of rCND-P0 compare to rCND-P300, rCND-P400. DLS measurement reveals an increase of particles size from 332 nm for CND-P to 747 nm for rCND-P300 (Fig. 4c). This increase is a result of the conversion of separated CND particles into an integrated graphene matrix. SEM images of rCND reconfirm the successful production of graphitic film (Fig. 4d and Fig. S12). Typical bubble-like compartments are observed for the 3D graphitic macrostructure. This unique shape is reported in previous publications [8,10]. Furthermore, focusing higher magnification on the edges of the graphitic sheets shows the layered structure of turbostratic graphitic films building the walls of the 3D matrix.

Laser graphitization has been successfully engaged for the last two decades. Great progress in the optimization of the process has been achieved. The chemical reaction is complex and hard to identify due to its rapidity and inhomogeneity. Nevertheless, it has been found that the mechanism of laser graphitization involves complex reaction that initiated by elimination of heteroatoms, mainly oxygen- and nitrogen-containing groups. At the same time, there are evidences on the importance of the structural properties of the precursors, especially involving aromatic and imide units [26]. For example, Tour et al. tried to laser-convert not less than fifteen different polymers, but came out with polyimides to from graphitic films [8]. Based on the results presented in this study, we believe that the chemical structure of the carbonaceous precursors should be considered in two aspects: A. the surface chemistry and B. the core structure.

Particle size separation. In this study we separated the products of the microwave reaction by mass using strong centrifugation (13 k rpm). While the large particles were easily identified in XRD and Raman two have large graphitic structure, the nature of the small particles, called in this study “supernatant” is still questionable. It could be nano-sized carbon nanodots or it may consist of organic molecules (such as citrazinic acid and HTTP). This study strengthens the small carbon dots conclusion. First, DLS measurements show nanoparticles with a mean diameter of 2.2 nm. For a nanoparticle to exist in the solution, it must exceed the critical radius, typically larger than 1.5 nm (due to too high surface tension). One can claim that the DLS detected an aggregation of the small molecules, however, we believe it is not likely for hydrophilic molecules to form stable aggregation for a long time and sonication. Second, XRD reveals a peak (only at) 2θ = 26⁰. This is typical for graphitic carbons, an indication of the graphitic cores of the CNDs. Indeed, solid aggregation of aromatic molecules can form this d-spacing, due to similar pi-pi stacking, however, one would expect these crystals would not be built only under strict conditions of gentle and long crystallization. Third, comparing the Raman spectra of CND-S0 and annealed CND-S300 and CND-S400 (Fig. S7), shows that upon annealing (meaning removal of functional groups on the surface of the particle) the broad D- and G-peaks of graphitic carbon remains, with similar ID/IG ratios. If it was only organic molecules, one would not see them.

Precarbonization. Specifically, in this study, we have shown that the precarbonization step strongly affects only the surface composition. This was directly shown by XPS, FTIR. The different surface chemistry is also reflected in the change in optical properties of the annealed samples. However, the structure of the core particle is not significantly affected by annealing. This conclusion is deduced from XRD, Raman, elemental analysis, and TGA results. A considerable difference in the core chemical structure was found between large and small CNDs: CND-S and CND-P. CND-S was found to have less graphitic structure. Laser treatment of this group of nanodots is unfruitful. Even after heat treatment (annealing), there was no successful conversion into graphitic film at any laser power. This observation teaches us that the surface chemistry is not the only factor determining effective laser reaction. On the other hand, the larger CND-P is more graphitic. Laser irradiation of this group of CNDs forms integrated graphitic films. In this regard, we have reported a noticeable effect of precarbonization. rCND-P300 demonstrates the best graphitization level, according to the ID/IG ratio (0.44); much better than unannealed rCND-P0 (1.08). This should be attributed to the higher carbon content on the surface induced by the heat. Since oxygen and nitrogen are extensively eliminated upon laser irradiation, over-concentrated heteroatoms lead to destructive decomposition and prevent the formation of sp2 carbon-carbon bonds. On the other hand, lasering CND-P400, annealed at higher temperatures, resulting in inferior laser product (ID/IG ratio of 0.83). Moreover, the mass loss of CND-P400 was higher than that of CND-P300. These observations point out that a too low concentration of oxygen and nitrogen on the surface suppresses the graphitization reaction. We can interpret that if the surface is free of oxygen- and nitrogen-containing groups, the surface is more stable, and laser irradiation does not initiate the graphitization process. This phenomenon was obtained in pure graphitic samples, such as carbon nanotubes (CNT) that did not overgo graphitization upon laser irradiation (not included in this report). Therefore, we conclude that two parameters are crucial for effective laser graphitization: graphitic/aromatic core and certain coverage of functional groups on the surface. According to the analysis of the chemical structure of the samples, the chemical composition of the surface required to achieve the optimal laser-induced 3D graphitic films contains 48.90% carbon 17.13% nitrogen and 30.21% oxygen. Moreover, the results are optimal when larger fraction of the carbon atoms are bonded to carbon atoms (68.5% C–C bonds in CND-P300). In this regard, it should be stated that this study is conducted on carbon nanodots (CND). Other carbonaceous precursors may behave differently, and further investigation should be undertaken to define the exact impact of precarbonization for each precursor.

Of course, the optimization parameters are relevant for this systems only. Other organic precursors such as graphene oxide, polyimides. MOF, and even other types of carbon nanodots, should have their own optimal parameters. Yet, we believe that the system investigated in this study faithfully represent the wide and growing groups of carbonaceous materials that can undergo laser graphitization. In regards to concept of chemical properties, surface and core structure, that are essential for the laser-induced reaction to occur.

In summary, we have found that while precarbonization affects only the surface chemistry and not the crystallinity of the precursors, the morphology of both the surface and the core plays in important role in the rapid and energetic thermal reaction. The surface functional groups initiate the laser reaction by exposing carbon atoms upon removal of surface groups. On the other hand, the crystal structure of the precursor is important to function as a nucleation seed for the growth of the graphitic lattice.

We have investigated the effect of precarbonization, i.e., prior heat treatment, on the final product of laser graphitization of carbon nanodots (CND). We have examined and characterized the samples at three synthesis points: First, after the microwave-assisted synthesis of the CNDs, the products were separated by mass. The smaller products, CND-S, were characterized by a high concentration of oxygen- and nitrogen-containing groups on the surface. On the other hand, the larger particles, CND-P, were identified by higher graphitization. Secondly, the samples were examined after annealing (heat treatment in an inert atmosphere) at different temperatures. The heat treatment altered mainly the surface chemistry of the samples but not their core structure. Finally, the samples were characterized as final products after the laser irradiation. For the ultimate goal, annealing the larger particles, rCND-P300, demonstrated the best graphitization level. All larger particles showed better compatibility with the laser-induced reaction. In fact, all CND-S samples failed to convert into graphitic matrix upon laser, regardless of the annealing temperature of laser power. Among the laser particles, CND-P, annealing at 300°C demonstrated the best ID/IG ratio of 0.44, compared to 1.08 of the not annealed CND-P0), proving the beneficial impact of precarbonization. When annealed to higher temperatures, although characterized with less oxygen- and nitrogen-containing groups on the surface, CND-P400 showed an inferior graphitization level (ID/IG ratio of 0.83). This suggests that the elimination of heteroatoms assists laser graphitization only to a certain extent. We postulate this observation as follows: Heteroatoms are crucial for the rapid thermal reaction to occur, as they are being eliminated and exposed carbon atoms in the graphitic core to engage in sp2 carbon-carbon bonds. On the other hand, too much of the functional group leads to fast decomposition of the sample and prevents the formation of the graphitic lattice.
Manish Kumar Yadav: Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Arie Borenstein: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the Israeli Ministry of Energy for supporting this research via grant no. 219-11-133.

ce:section.ce:para: The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
